steps.title: Identify what the blank must do
steps.content: The sentence reads: "This type of random, continuous ______ is known as Brownian motion, can be observed throughout the natural world." The clause "which is known as Brownian motion" is a nonessential (extra-information) relative clause — it can be removed without breaking the sentence. Nonessential relative clauses must be enclosed by matching punctuation: a comma before "which" and the existing comma after "motion" are both required.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'movement: which': A colon can only follow an independent clause; "This type of random, continuous movement" is not independent. Also, colons do not set off nonessential relative clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — 'movement, which': Supplies the required comma before "which," completing the pair with the comma already after "motion." The nonessential clause is properly enclosed. ✓
  • Choice C — 'movement which': No comma before "which," so the nonessential clause is missing its opening punctuation mark. ✗
  • Choice D — 'movement. Which': A period ends the sentence at "movement," leaving "Which is known as Brownian motion, can be observed throughout the natural world" as a fragment. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
